title: Why American Sperm Count Dropped 41% in 50 years
summary: Dr. Shanna Swan, a reproductive epidemiologist at Mount Sinai, discusses her research linking toxic chemicals like phthalates and pesticides to dramatic declines in male sperm count (down 41% since the 1970s) and testosterone levels. The interview covers how these endocrine-disrupting chemicals affect fetal development, fertility rates, and long-term health outcomes, while exploring potential solutions and the regulatory failures allowing these exposures.

acronyms.definition: The distance between the anus and genitals, used as a proxy indicator for testosterone exposure during fetal development and predictor of sperm count

acronyms.expansion: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
acronyms.definition: U.S. federal health agency that can measure chemical exposure in people's bodies
U.S. federal health research agency that issued requirements for including women in studies
A procedure where a single sperm is injected directly into an egg for fertilization
Political and economic union of European countries with stricter chemical regulations than the US

events.description: Study published from Denmark showing sperm count had declined precipitously
Dr. Swan began definitively linking phthalates and pesticides to declining male sperm count and testosterone
Study showed average sperm count dropped to 47 million per milliliter
Peak baby boom in the US with fertility rate at 3.58

key_claims.claim: Male sperm count has dropped to 59% of what it was in the 1970s
Testosterone has been dropping 1% per year since the 1970s
Men in central Missouri had half as many moving sperm as men in Minneapolis
Below 40 million sperm per milliliter, men start having trouble conceiving
In the EU, 1100 chemicals are banned from personal care products versus only 11 in the US
Phthalates in pregnant women during early first trimester can lower fetal testosterone and affect development
World population will increase until about 2040 and then decrease and never come back
South Korea's fertility rate is at 0.9
US fertility rate is now 1.78, below replacement rate
Until about five years ago, there was no requirement that women be included in medical studies
